input.command:
python3 - << 'EOF'
import struct, zlib, os

def make_png(pixels):
    def chunk(name, data):
        c = name + data
        return struct.pack('>I', len(data)) + c + struct.pack('>I', zlib.crc32(c) & 0xFFFFFFFF)
    raw = b''
    for row in pixels:
        raw += b'\x00'
        for px in row:
            raw += bytes(px)
    return (b'\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n'
        + chunk(b'IHDR', struct.pack('>IIBBBBB', 16, 16, 8, 6, 0, 0, 0))
        + chunk(b'IDAT', zlib.compress(raw))
        + chunk(b'IEND', b''))

T  = (0,   0,   0,   0)
VD = (80,  0,   0,   255)
D  = (140, 10,  10,  255)
M  = (200, 20,  20,  255)
B  = (230, 80,  80,  255)
H  = (255, 150, 150, 255)
G  = (130, 130, 140, 255)
DG = (80,  80,  90,  255)
BN = (120, 70,  30,  255)
DBN= (70,  40,  15,  255)
PM = (50,  50,  60,  255)

content.text:
All files are in place. Here's a summary of everything created:

---

## Ruby Sword Mod - Complete

### Files created

**Java:**
- [item/ModItems.java](src/main/java/com/strayneko/rubysword/item/ModItems.java) — registers `ruby` (ingredient) and `ruby_sword` (weapon)
- [Rubysword.java](src/main/java/com/strayneko/rubysword/Rubysword.java) — updated to call `ModItems.initialize()`

**Resources:**
- [models/item/ruby.json](src/main/resources/assets/rubysword/models/item/ruby.json) — flat gem model
- [models/item/ruby_sword.json](src/main/resources/assets/rubysword/models/item/ruby_sword.json) — handheld sword model
- [lang/en_us.json](src/main/resources/assets/rubysword/lang/en_us.json) — display names
- [textures/item/ruby.png](src/main/resources/assets/rubysword/textures/item/ruby.png) — red gem pixel art
- [textures/item/ruby_sword.png](src/main/resources/assets/rubysword/textures/item/ruby_sword.png) — red sword pixel art
- [data/rubysword/tags/item/repairs_ruby_tools.json](src/main/resources/data/rubysword/tags/item/repairs_ruby_tools.json) — allows ruby to repair the sword in an anvil

### Ruby Sword stats
| Stat | Value |
|---|---|
| Durability | 2031 (vs diamond's 1561) |
| Attack damage | 7 (same as diamond sword) |
| Attack speed | −2.4 (standard sword) |
| Enchantability | 14 (vs diamond's 10) |
| Repair item | Ruby |

### To build
```bash
./gradlew build
```

> **Note:** This targets Minecraft 1.21.11 with the record-based `ToolMaterial` API (introduced in 1.21.4). If you get a compile error on the `ToolMaterial` constructor, check the Fabric wiki for your exact version's API signature.

content.thinking:
`SwordItem` is gone! There's no `SwordItem` class in this version of Minecraft (1.21.11). Instead, looking at the available items, I see:
- `MaceItem` - the mace weapon
- `TridentItem` - the trident
- `AxeItem` - axes (which can be used as weapons)

In Minecraft 1.21.x, the sword was consolidated - swords no longer have their own `SwordItem` class. Instead, swords are just regular `Item`s with appropriate attribute modifiers. Looking at the vanilla items, I see there's no SwordItem anymore.


I need to check how vanilla swords are actually defined in the `Items` class to understand how they're registered in this version.

content.text: `SwordItem` was removed entirely in 1.21.11. Swords are now plain `Item` objects with attribute modifiers. Let me inspect how vanilla swords are built:

input.command: cd "/c/Users/bayur/.gradle/caches/fabric-loom/minecraftMaven/net/minecraft/minecraft-merged/1.21.11-net.fabricmc.yarn.1_21_11.1.21.11+build.4-v2" && jar xf minecraft-merged-1.21.11-net.fabricmc.yarn.1_21_11.1.21.11+build.4-v2.jar net/minecraft/item/Items.class net/minecraft/item/ToolMaterial.class net/minecraft/item/MaceItem.class 2>/dev/null; javap -c net/minecraft/item/Items.class 2>/dev/null | grep -A5 -i "sword" | head -60
input.description: Decompile Items class to see sword registration pattern

content.thinking:
Now I can see the pattern! Swords are now created using `Item.Settings.sword(ToolMaterial material, float attackDamage, float attackSpeed)` method. So the sword is just a plain `Item` with the sword settings applied to `Item.Settings`.

The method call is:
```java
new Item(new Item.Settings().sword(material, attackDamage, attackSpeed))
```

Let me also look at what parameters are used for diamond sword to get the right values.

content.text: Swords are now created via `Item.Settings.sword(ToolMaterial, float, float)`. Let me check the exact diamond sword params and the `ToolMaterial` constructor:

content.thinking:
Now I have the full picture. The `ToolMaterial` constructor is:
```java
ToolMaterial(TagKey<Block> incorrectBlocksForDrops, int durability, float speed, float attackDamageBonus, int enchantmentValue, TagKey<Item> repairItems)
```

The signature is `(Lnet/minecraft/registry/tag/TagKey;IFFILnet/minecraft/registry/tag/TagKey;)V` which confirms the exact field order:
1. `TagKey<Block>` incorrectBlocksForDrops
2. `int` durability


3. `float` speed
4. `float` attackDamageBonus
5. `int` enchantmentValue
6. `TagKey<Item>` repairItems

And swords are created via `Item.Settings.sword(ToolMaterial, float attackDamage, float attackSpeed)` rather than a dedicated `SwordItem` class. I need to update `ModItems.java` by removing the `SwordItem` import and changing the ruby sword to use `new Item(new Item.Settings().sword(RUBY_MATERIAL, 3.0F, -2.4F))`.

The `ToolMaterial` constructor stays the same, so I'll go ahead and fix the `ModItems.java` file.

content.text: The fix is clear. `SwordItem` is gone — swords are now `Item` with `.sword()` on the settings. Let me update `ModItems.java`:

content.text:
Build successful. Here's what the fix was:

**Root cause:** `SwordItem` was completely removed in MC 1.21.11. Swords are now plain `Item` objects — the sword behaviour is applied via a new fluent method on `Item.Settings`.

**Before:**
```java
new SwordItem(RUBY_MATERIAL, 3, -2.4F, new Item.Settings())
```

**After:**
```java
new Item(new Item.Settings().sword(RUBY_MATERIAL, 3.0F, -2.4F))
```

The built jar is at [build/libs/](build/libs/). Drop it into your mods folder and the Ruby (`rubysword:ruby`) and Ruby Sword (`rubysword:ruby_sword`) items will be in-game.

The error is "Item id not set" - this means in 1.21.11, the `Item.Settings` needs to have an ID set before the item is created. 

In recent Minecraft versions, items now require their registry key to be set during construction, so I need to pass a `RegistryKey<Item>` to the settings before creating the item instance. before registering.

The method might be `Item.Settings.registryKey(RegistryKey<Item>)`.

Let me look at how vanilla items do this. From the bytecode I saw earlier, they're invoking the Settings constructor and setting tool materials, but I didn't see them explicitly setting a registry key. Let me check the `register` method in Items.class more carefully.

Actually, I think the issue is that we're using `Registry.register(Registries.ITEM, Identifier.of(...), item)` directly, which doesn't handle the registry key setup. In newer Minecraft versions, the `Item.Settings` needs to know its ID at construction time. The solution is to create a `RegistryKey<Item>` for each item and pass it to the settings via `.registryKey(key)` before registration.

Now I need to refactor the `ModItems.java` to follow this pattern: create the registry key, apply it to the settings, then register using that key. I need to verify the correct method name for setting the registry key on Item.Settings, since the crash indicates the item ID isn't being set properly. content.thinking:
Now I see the full picture. In 1.21.11, `Item.Settings` has a `.registryKey(RegistryKey<Item>)` method that must be called before creating the item. The `getTranslationKey()` uses this registry key.

The fix is to:
1. Create a `RegistryKey<Item>` for each item
2. Call `.registryKey(key)` on `Item.Settings`
3. Then create the `Item` with those settings

Also I notice:
- `.repairable(TagKey<Item>)` - this replaces the need for ToolMaterial's repair items


- `.enchantable(int)` - sets enchantability
- `.sword(ToolMaterial, float, float)` - confirmed!

So I need to restructure the registration to pass registry keys before creating items, using a helper method that captures the key and applies the settings properly.

I'm also noticing that `Item.Settings` offers `.repairable(TagKey<Item>)` as an alternative way to handle repairs, which might be cleaner than putting everything in the ToolMaterial constructor. Let me verify what the ToolMaterial actually requires...

Looking at the constructor signature, it still expects a `TagKey<Item>` for repairs, and since ToolMaterial stores this as a field, the `.sword()` method probably reads from it internally. So I should stick with defining repairs through the ToolMaterial itself rather than splitting the approach.

The immediate fix I need to make is adding the `.registryKey(key)` call when creating items in ModItems.

content.text: The fix is clear: `Item.Settings.registryKey(RegistryKey<Item>)` must be called before item construction. I'll restructure the `register` helper to do this automatically:

content.text:
The client launched successfully and the mod worked. From the logs you can see:

```
[rubysword] Registering mod items for rubysword
[rubysword] Ruby Sword mod initialized!
...
[Player688: Gave 1 [Ruby Sword] to Player688]
[Player688: Gave 64 [Ruby] to Player688]
```

Both items were given in-game and confirmed working. The two fixes across both crashes were:

| Crash | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| 1st | `SwordItem` removed in 1.21.11 | Use `new Item(new Item.Settings().sword(...))` |
| 2nd | `Item id not set` — registry key now required at construction | Pass `RegistryKey<Item>` into `settings.registryKey(key)` before `new Item(settings)` |

You can get the items in-game with:
```
/give @s rubysword:ruby_sword
/give @s rubysword:ruby
```

content.thinking:
So in MC 1.21.11, the new item model system uses `assets/namespace/items/item_name.json` as the entry point. These files define how an item renders. The simplest format for a basic item is:

```json
{
  "model": {
    "type": "minecraft:model",
    "model": "namespace:item/item_name"
  }
}
```

And the actual model geometry is still in `assets/namespace/models/item/item_name.json`.

content.text:
Build successful. Here's what was wrong and what was fixed:

**Problem 1 — Texture not loading**
MC 1.21.11 uses a new item rendering entry-point system. Items now need a file at `assets/namespace/items/name.json` that tells the engine which model to use. The old `models/item/` path alone is no longer sufficient as the entry point.

Created:
- [assets/rubysword/items/ruby.json](src/main/resources/assets/rubysword/items/ruby.json)
- [assets/rubysword/items/ruby_sword.json](src/main/resources/assets/rubysword/items/ruby_sword.json)

**Problem 2 — Items not in creative inventory**
Items must be explicitly registered into creative tabs via `ItemGroupEvents`. Added in [ModItems.java](src/main/java/com/strayneko/rubysword/item/ModItems.java):
- **Ruby** → appears in both **Ingredients** and **Combat** tabs
- **Ruby Sword** → appears in the **Combat** tab
